phase_metadata.description:
Transition from imperative to
functional programming style using Lambda Expressions, the Stream API, and
Optional class to write concise, readable, and robust code.

learning_objectives:
Understand the core concepts of Functional
Programming in Java
Master Lambda Expressions and Functional Interfaces
Perform complex data processing using the Stream API (Filter, Map, Reduce)
Use Method References to write cleaner code

Eliminate NullPointerExceptions
using the Optional class
Understand Default and Static methods in interfaces
introduction:
Java 8 marked a
revolutionary shift in the language by introducing Functional Programming
capabilities. Before this, Java was strictly imperative (telling the computer
'how' to do things step-by-step). With Lambdas and Streams, we can program
declaratively (telling the computer 'what' we want to achieve). This results in
code that is more concise, easier to parallelize, and significantly more
readable.

concept_explanation.what_is_it:
Functional programming in
Java revolves around treating code as data. A 'Lambda Expression' is an
anonymous method (no name, no return type) that provides an implementation for a
Functional Interface. The 'Stream API' is a pipeline of operations used to
process collections of objects.
concept_explanation.functional_interfaces:
A Functional
Interface is an interface that has exactly one abstract method (SAM - Single
Abstract Method). Examples include Runnable, Comparator, and the built-in ones
in java.util.function like Predicate (returns boolean) and Consumer (takes
input, returns nothing).
concept_explanation.stream_api:
A Stream is not a data structure (it
doesn't store data). It is a sequence of elements from a source (like a List)
that supports aggregate operations. Operations are either Intermediate
(transform the stream, e.g., filter, map) or Terminal (produce a result, e.g.,
collect, forEach).
concept_explanation.optional_class:
Optional is a container object which may
or may not contain a non-null value. It forces the developer to handle the case
where a value might be missing, reducing the risk of NullPointerExceptions.
concept_explanation.method_references:
A shorthand notation for a lambda expression that only
calls an existing method. Syntax: ClassName::methodName.

syntax_breakdown.arrow_operator: Separates the parameter list from the lambda body.
syntax_breakdown.predicate:
A functional interface method that takes one argument and returns
a boolean (used in .filter()).
syntax_breakdown.function:
A functional interface method that
takes one argument and returns a result (used in .map()).
syntax_breakdown.terminal_operation:
An operation that triggers the processing of the stream.
Once called, the stream is consumed and cannot be reused.
syntax_breakdown.lazy_evaluation:
Intermediate stream operations are not performed until a terminal operation is
invoked.

rules_and_key_points:
A lambda can only be used with
Functional Interfaces.

Variables used inside a lambda must be 'effectively
final' (their value doesn't change after initialization).

Streams do not
modify the original source; they produce a new result.

Intermediate
operations are 'lazy'; they don't execute until a terminal operation is
called.
A Stream can be traversed only once.

Parallel streams can be used
for performance gains on large data sets (stream().parallel()).
Optional.get() should be avoided; use orElse() or ifPresent() instead.

Default methods in interfaces allow adding new functionality without breaking
existing implementations.

The :: syntax (Method Reference) is preferred over
lambdas for simplicity when calling existing methods.

Collectors.toList() or
the newer .toList() (Java 16+) converts streams back to lists.

code_example_1.title: Lambda and Method Reference Basics
code_example_1.description:
Implementing a custom functional interface and using method
references with forEach.

code_example_2.title: Stream API and Optional in Action
code_example_2.description: Filtering, transforming, and collecting data from a list of objects.
code_example_2.code: E-commerce: Filtering products by category, price range, and rating.

Data
Analysis: Calculating averages or sums of large datasets from CSV files.

API
Development: Transforming Database Entities into DTOs (Data Transfer Objects).
Security: Filtering a list of permissions based on user roles.

Logging:
Processing log files to find specific error patterns.

common_mistakes:
Calling more than one terminal operation on the same stream (causes
IllegalStateException).

Using streams for simple loops where a traditional
for-loop is faster and clearer.

Modifying the underlying collection inside a
stream operation (causes Side Effects).

Using Optional.get() without checking
ifPresent() (leads back to NullPointerException).

Forgetting the terminal
operation (the stream pipeline won't execute at all).
best_practices:
Prefer method references (String::toUpperCase) over lambdas (s ->
s.toUpperCase()) where possible.

Keep lambdas short. If a lambda is more
than 3 lines, move it to a separate method.

Always use the
@FunctionalInterface annotation to clarify intent and prevent errors.

Use
Optional for return types, not as method parameters or class fields.

Use
primitive streams (IntStream, DoubleStream) to avoid the overhead of
boxing/unboxing.

faq.question:
Can I use a lambda with an abstract
class that has one method?
faq.answer:
No. Lambdas only work with interfaces.
Abstract classes, even with one method, are not functional interfaces.

faq.question: What is the difference between map() and flatMap()?
faq.answer:
map() transforms each element into another element. flatMap() transforms each
element into a stream and then flattens all those streams into a single stream
(useful for nested collections).

faq.question:
Why is the variable in a
lambda required to be effectively final?
faq.answer:
Lambdas may be executed in
different threads. To avoid concurrency issues and ensure consistency, Java
prevents lambdas from modifying local variables from the enclosing scope.

code_example_1.summary:
Phase 12 introduced the power of Functional Programming in Java. We
learned how Lambda Expressions simplify code, how the Stream API allows for
powerful data processing pipelines, and how the Optional class helps us write
safer, null-free code.
key_takeaways:
Functional Interfaces have exactly
one abstract method.
Lambdas replace bulky anonymous inner classes.
Streams provide a declarative way to process collections.

Intermediate
operations are lazy; Terminal operations trigger execution.

Optional is a
container to safely handle missing values.

Method references make code
cleaner by reusing existing methods.
Streams do not change the source data.
Filter is for selection; Map is for transformation.

Collectors transform
streams back into collections.

Functional code is often easier to read and
maintain.
code_example_1.preview_of_the_next_topic:
In Phase 13, we will dive into
Multithreading, learning how to make our Java programs perform multiple tasks
simultaneously.

questions.question: What is a Functional Interface in Java?
questions.answer:
A Functional
Interface is an interface that contains exactly one abstract method. It can
contain multiple default or static methods, but only one 'unimplemented' method.
This allows it to be the target for a lambda expression.

questions.question:
Explain 'Lazy Evaluation' in the
context of Java Streams.
questions.answer:
Lazy Evaluation means that intermediate
operations (like filter or map) are not executed as soon as they are defined.
Instead, they are recorded as a pipeline of tasks. The actual execution happens
only when a terminal operation (like collect or count) is called, allowing the
JVM to optimize the processing.

questions.question:
Write a stream pipeline that takes a list of
integers, squares each number, and finds the sum of the squared values.
questions.answer: int sum = list.stream().map(n -> n * n).reduce(0, Integer::sum);

questions.question:
Given a List of
Strings, write a stream to find the first string that starts with 'J' and has a
length greater than 5. Return it as an Optional.
questions.answer:
The bug is that the stream 's' is used twice. Streams cannot be
reused after a terminal operation (forEach) has been called. The call to
s.count() will throw an IllegalStateException.

Variables used in a lambda
must be 'final' or 'effectively final'. Here, 'total' is a local variable being
modified inside the lambda, which is not allowed. To fix this, use a Stream
reduce or a specialized primitive stream (IntStream).

questions.question:
Write a lambda expression for the
Comparator interface to sort a List of Strings by their length.

Implement a custom Functional Interface 'MathOp'
that takes two integers and returns an integer. Use it to perform a
multiplication using a lambda.
